evidence: The docstring asks how large the limsup of |A ∩ {1,…,N}|/N^{1/2} can be for a Sidon set A ⊆ ℕ (module header: 'Maximum Density of Sidon Sets').
Docstring: Erdős proved there exists a Sidon set whose √N-normalized upper density is at least 1/2.
Docstring: Krückeberg [Kr61] exhibited an infinite Sidon set with sidonUpperDensity equal to 1/√2.
Docstring: Erdős and Turán [ErTu41] proved every Sidon set has √N-normalized upper density at most 1 ('On a problem of Sidon in additive number theory').
The statement conditions on the maximum upper density of Sidon sets being 1 and concludes an embedding property for finite Sidon sets.
The conclusion asserts every finite Sidon set embeds in a perfect difference set modulo n (IsPerfectDifferenceSet), citing counterexamples in [Ha47] on cyclic projective planes and [AlMi25].
AdditiveBasisCondition requires every integer to be n^2 + a with a ∈ A, i.e. A is an additive complement/basis for the squares; erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'additive basis'.
Docstring: Erdős observed the minimal limsup counting value over sets satisfying AdditiveBasisCondition is finite and greater than 1.
Docstring: van Doorn bounded the smallest limsup value over additive-basis sets A by 2φ^(5/2).
The statement asks for a minimal asymptotic additive basis A of order h (MinAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder); erdosproblems.com tags the problem 'additive basis'.
Docstring: the basis must have positive density and, for each n ∈ A, the upper density of integers not representable without n must be positive (HasPosDensity).
Docstring defines HasBoundedGaps as 'syndetic, meaning there is a uniform bound M such that every interval of length M contains an element of S', and asks when D(A) has bounded gaps.
Docstring: for any permutation π ∈ S_n of {1,…,n}, S(π) counts distinct consecutive sums; asks whether S(π) = o(n^2) for all π (Hegyvári gave a counterexample).
The greedy Sidon sequence A = {1, 2, 4, 8, 13, 21, 31, 45, …} of the docstring is the Mian–Chowla sequence (OEIS A005282, listed for problem 340 on erdosproblems.com).
Docstring: the sequence is built by greedily including the next integer that preserves the Sidon property (no non-trivial solutions to a + b = c + d).
Asks for the exact order of growth of the greedy Sidon (Mian–Chowla, OEIS A005282) sequence's counting function.
Docstring: Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] asked whether the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence has positive density.
Docstring: Erdős and Graham [ErGr80] asked whether 22 is in the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence, which it is.
Docstring: 33 is the smallest integer not known to be in the difference set A − A of the greedy Sidon sequence.
Docstring: it may be true that all or almost all integers (here: all but finitely many) are in A − A for the greedy Sidon sequence.
Docstring: it may be true that almost all integers (here: outside a set of density zero) are in A − A for the greedy Sidon sequence.
Docstring asks whether infinitely many pairs (a, a+2) occur in Ulam's sequence (IsUlamSequence, OEIS A002858).
Docstring asks whether Ulam's sequence eventually has periodic differences a(n+1) − a(n).
Docstring part (iii) asks whether the density of Ulam's sequence is 0.
The statement asserts Set.upperDensity (Set.range a) = 0, i.e. the asymptotic density of the Ulam sequence vanishes.
